Paul's extended list of greetings in this chapter might seem like the least important part of his letter to the Romans—a mere formality tacked on at the end. Yet what unfolds here is something far more significant: the concrete reality of the church as a living, breathing community of actual people. These are not abstractions. Phoebe is a deacon who has helped Paul. Prisca and Aquila risked their lives for him. Epenetus was the first convert in Asia. Mary, Tryphena, Tryphosa, and Persis worked hard. Rufus's mother cared for Paul like a son. What Paul has taught throughout Romans about grace, righteousness, and the Spirit is not merely theology—it is the force that binds together this messy collection of former pagans, former Jews, slaves, freed people, women leaders, and fellow laborers. The gospel produces not isolated believers but a family. When Paul urges the Romans to greet one another with a holy kiss and to welcome Phoebe in a manner worthy of the saints, he is reminding them that receiving the grace of Christ means receiving one another.

But this chapter contains a warning that cuts against any romanticized view of church life. Even as Paul celebrates the faithful, he demands vigilance against those who create divisions and deceive through smooth talk and flattery. These false teachers are not pursuing Christ's agenda but their own appetites, drawing naive people away from what the Romans have been taught. The church is not naturally unified. The body of Christ requires discernment, resistance, and sometimes separation. Paul calls the Romans to be "wise about what is good and innocent about what is evil"—not naive, but also not entangled with deception. This is the work of building a real community: celebrating faithful workers, protecting the vulnerable from seduction, and turning away from those who undermine Christ's teaching.

Paul closes with an affirmation that reaches beyond Rome to the cosmic victory that is already unfolding. The God of peace will crush Satan under their feet—not in some distant future alone, but even now, as they live in obedience rooted in faith. The grace of Christ is with them. Then Paul's final words orient the reader away from himself and toward the eternal God, whose purpose to bring all nations into the obedience of faith is now revealed through Christ and confirmed in the prophets. Glory belongs to the only wise God, forever, through Jesus Christ. What begins as a catalog of names ends in doxology. This is how the church lives: it is made up of real people doing real work, standing against real deception, and participating in real victory—all under the Lordship of Christ and the reign of the only wise God.
